bibliographic.citation: Geoffrey Chaucer, The Ellesmere MS. of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, ed. Frederick J. Furnivall, Chaucer Society, First Series nos. 2, 8, 16, 26, 32, 38, 50, 70 (London: N. Trübner, 1868-1884; final title page 1868-1879), x, [3], 683 [i.e. 687], 28* pp., 3 leaves, 46 plates; Flügel correction leaf, 1907.
bibliographic.title: The Ellesmere MS. of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales

entities.description: The immediate printed source for the U-M encoding: a Chaucer Society diplomatic-style print of the Ellesmere text with separately sourced appendices, twenty-three Hooper woodcut teller drawings, twenty-three manuscript-miniature facsimiles, and a 1907 correction leaf. It was issued in eight parts and later bound in varying carrier configurations.
entities.label: Furnivall's one-manuscript Ellesmere edition, 1868-1884

rights.basis: The edition and all identified components were published no later than 1907, well before the U.S. pre-1931 public-domain cutoff in force in 2026.
rights.component: Published medieval text, Furnivall editorial matter, Hooper woodcuts, miniature plates, typography, appendices, and 1907 Flügel corrections

rights.basis: The cached official GND authority record identifies the editor as Frederick James Furnivall and gives his death date as 2 July 1910, so life-plus-70 copyright in his published contribution expired; the edition's typographical arrangement also exceeds the United Kingdom's 25-year term. This assertion is limited to the published edition and does not decide rights in otherwise unpublished manuscript variants.
rights.component: Furnivall's published edition text and expired typographical arrangement

access.accessMethod: Huntington Library research access subject to current reader and handling rules; use the digital surrogate where possible
access.entityLabel: Huntington Library MS EL 26 C 9 (Ellesmere Chaucer)

rights.basis: Copyright status does not attach to ownership of the physical manuscript object. Huntington's custody, reading-room rules, handling requirements, and reproduction-service terms are separate access and property layers.
rights.component: Physical Ellesmere codex as a material object
rights.jurisdiction: Copyright layer; physical property and reading-room terms are separate

entities.description: Deluxe illuminated manuscript of The Canterbury Tales, produced in England c. 1400-1410. It is the principal manuscript source of Furnivall's edition and contains twenty-three teller miniatures, including a likely portrait of Chaucer.

holding.notes: The current digital item records 1995 conservation and directs use through the Huntington Digital Library.

entities.label: Huntington Library MS EL 26 C 9 (Ellesmere Chaucer)

physicalDescription.extent: ii modern + iv contemporary + 232 main folios + iv modern + ii contemporary
notes: The official digital record catalogs the manuscript c. 1400-1410 and describes twenty-two pilgrim miniatures plus a likely Chaucer portrait.

rights.basis: These exact medieval textual and artistic components were demonstrably published in Furnivall's edition no later than 1907; the edition is public domain under the cited United States publication rule and United Kingdom term analysis.
rights.component: Exact Ellesmere Canterbury Tales text and twenty-three teller miniatures published in Furnivall's edition
rights.jurisdiction: United States and United Kingdom historical-work analysis
notes: This does not assert a blanket United Kingdom status for any Ellesmere content or variants not shown to have been published before 1989; such material is outside this record and may require separate analysis of the possible 2039 term.

rights.basis: Copyright status does not attach to ownership of the physical manuscript object. Huntington's custody, reading-room rules, handling requirements, and reproduction-service terms are separate access and property layers.
rights.component: Physical Ellesmere codex as a material object
rights.jurisdiction: Copyright layer; physical property and reading-room terms are separate

next_steps: Treat the current item statement as the provider's present designation.
Preserve the older embedded notice and avoid inferring that the CME software Unlicense covers XML/data.
Seek written U-M clarification before high-risk redistribution of the encoding as a standalone dataset.
openQuestions.question: How should the current U-M No Copyright-United States statement interact with the embedded 2006 copyright/restrictions notice for redistribution of OCR and markup?

next_steps: Use the public viewer and stable PB inventory for current access claims.
Do not describe a bulk image download until an exact documented endpoint and terms page are verified.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M expose a documented bulk download for the exact 846 page images, and what item-specific terms attach to that route?

next_steps: Retain Furnivall's shelfmark-level source claim at medium confidence.
Search the current Bodleian Archives and Manuscripts catalogue by shelfmark before any witness-level image request.

next_steps: Keep HathiTrust records as access routes rather than audited complete carriers.

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is one composite work representation, not 846 page-image works and not an undifferentiated physical-plus-digital object.

Exactly twenty-four tales are counted in metadata. The Gamelyn appendix, supplementary links, rejected Clerk link, Hooper woodcuts, manuscript-miniature facsimiles, Gg.4.27 drawing appendix, and 1907 Flügel correction leaf remain visible as non-counted apparatus.

The Internet Archive/Google carrier is a discontiguous partial scan: it ends the main text during the Second Nun's Tale, then supplies Gamelyn and publisher matter; it nevertheless includes all twenty-three Hooper cuts and the Flügel leaf but no separate twenty-three-miniature facsimile suite.

The Wikimedia Commons DLI carrier is also partial relative to the complete edition: it contains the full main text through the Parson's Tale and Retraction, Gamelyn, the rejected Clerk link, all twenty-three Hooper cuts, and Parson analysis, but omits appendix pp. 27*-28*, the Gg.4.27 drawings, the twenty-three miniature facsimiles, and the Flügel leaf.

The U-M current No Copyright-United States statement, its embedded 2006 restrictions notice, Google scan-use notice, BSB NoC-NC statements, Huntington policy, CUDL's separate image and metadata licenses, public-domain status of the nineteenth-century edition, physical-object non-applicability, and the CME repository's software-worded Unlicense are scoped separately rather than merged.

lineage.summary: The General Corpus artifact is CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/agz8232.xml, a pinned copy of University of Michigan item AGZ8232.0001.001. U-M created the uncorrected OCR/Level-1 encoding in 2006 from Frederick J. Furnivall's Chaucer Society one-manuscript edition, issued in eight parts from 1868 to 1884. Furnivall transcribed the Canterbury Tales core principally from Huntington Library MS EL 26 C 9, while explicitly sourcing appendix texts from British Library Royal 18 C II, Bodleian Arch. Selden B.14, and Cambridge Dd.4.24 and reproducing drawings from Cambridge Gg.4.27. Official Huntington, BSB, and CUDL IIIF presentations, U-M page images, and audited Internet Archive/Google and Wikimedia Commons scans are modeled as distinct carriers with carrier-specific coverage and rights; cached GND and U.S. Copyright Office records support, but do not contain, the target work.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer; anonymous or uncertain responsibility for non-Ellesmere appendix texts
catalogSummary.date: Composed chiefly in the late 1380s and 1390s; Ellesmere manuscript c. 1400-1410; source edition issued 1868-1884
catalogSummary.region: England; associated with late-medieval London literary language
catalogSummary.title: The Canterbury Tales

catalogingSubject.label: The Canterbury Tales (Ellesmere/Furnivall representation)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame and twenty-four-tale collection as transmitted principally in Huntington Library MS EL 26 C 9 (the Ellesmere Chaucer) and represented by Frederick J. Furnivall's one-manuscript Chaucer Society edition. The cataloged unit includes the edition's visible witness-specific and editorial apparatus but does not count the non-Ellesmere Gamelyn and links, rejected Clerk link, illustration suites, or correction leaf as Chaucerian tales. The physical manuscript, printed edition, digital facsimiles, U-M encoding, and repository XML remain distinct lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Group A, Fragment I
contentParts.summary: General Prologue and the Knight, Miller, Reeve, and incomplete Cook sequence.

contentParts.label: General Prologue
contentParts.summary: Pilgrimage frame, pilgrim portraits, and tale-telling agreement.

contentParts.label: The Knight's Tale
contentParts.summary: The Knight's verse romance of Palamon, Arcite, and Emelye.

contentParts.label: Miller's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Miller interrupts the planned social order of telling.

contentParts.label: The Miller's Tale
contentParts.summary: Fabliau of Nicholas, Alisoun, Absolon, and John.

contentParts.label: Reeve's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Reeve answers the Miller and introduces his retaliatory tale.

contentParts.label: The Reeve's Tale
contentParts.summary: Fabliau of the miller Symkyn and two Cambridge clerks.

contentParts.label: Cook's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Cook reacts to the Reeve's tale and begins his own.

contentParts.label: The Cook's Tale
notes: The separately sourced Tale of Gamelyn is retained only in the appendix and is not used to complete this tale.
contentParts.summary: Incomplete fabliau of Perkyn Revel.

contentParts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
contentParts.summary: Man of Law head-link, prologue, and tale.

contentParts.label: Man of Law's Head-link
contentParts.summary: Host's timekeeping and summons to the Man of Law.

contentParts.label: Man of Law's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Man of Law introduces his tale and comments on Chaucer's writings.

contentParts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale of Constance's exile, trials, and restoration.

contentParts.label: Group D, Fragment V
contentParts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence.

contentParts.label: Wife of Bath's Preamble
contentParts.summary: The Wife's autobiographical and argumentative prologue.

contentParts.label: Friar-Summoner Interruption
contentParts.summary: The Friar and Summoner interrupt the Wife's preamble and quarrel.

contentParts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
contentParts.summary: Arthurian romance of a knight learning what women most desire.

contentParts.label: Friar's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Friar prepares a satirical tale against summoners.

contentParts.label: The Friar's Tale
contentParts.summary: A corrupt summoner encounters a devil.

contentParts.label: Summoner's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Summoner answers the Friar with an anti-fraternal preface.

contentParts.label: The Summoner's Tale
contentParts.summary: Fabliau and clerical satire of Friar John and Thomas.

contentParts.label: Summoner's Tale Fart-distribution Coda
contentParts.summary: The squire's proposed geometric division of Thomas's gift.

contentParts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
contentParts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence; the Squire prologue begins at the group's end and continues in Group F.

contentParts.label: Clerk's Head-link
contentParts.summary: The Host calls upon the Clerk to tell.

contentParts.label: The Clerk's Tale
contentParts.summary: The Clerk's six-part tale of Griselda, followed by Chaucer's envoy.

contentParts.label: Rejected Clerk End-link
notes: Excluded from the 17,353-line core metric and from counted tales.
contentParts.summary: Seven-line link printed by Furnivall as possibly original but rejected apparatus.

contentParts.label: Merchant's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Merchant reacts to the Clerk and introduces his own marital tale.

contentParts.label: The Merchant's Tale
contentParts.summary: January, May, and Damyan in a marriage tale with fabliau elements.

contentParts.label: Group F, Fragment VII
contentParts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence.

contentParts.label: Squire's Head-link and Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host turns to the Squire; Furnivall's encoding splits the continuous introductory unit across the end of Group E and start of Group F.

contentParts.label: The Squire's Tale
contentParts.summary: Incomplete romance of Cambyuskan and marvelous gifts.

contentParts.label: Squire-Franklin Link
contentParts.summary: The Franklin praises the Squire and the Host calls on the Franklin.

contentParts.label: Franklin's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Franklin disclaims rhetorical learning before his Breton lay.

contentParts.label: The Franklin's Tale
contentParts.summary: Breton lay of Dorigen, Arveragus, Aurelius, and competing promises.

contentParts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
contentParts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence.

contentParts.label: The Physician's Tale
contentParts.summary: Moral tragedy of Virginia and Apius.

contentParts.label: Physician-Pardoner Link
contentParts.summary: The Host responds to the Physician's tale and calls on the Pardoner.

contentParts.label: Pardoner's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Pardoner exposes his fraudulent preaching practice.

contentParts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
contentParts.summary: Exemplum of three rioters who seek Death and destroy one another.

contentParts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
contentParts.summary: Shipman through Nun's Priest sequence in the Ellesmere/Furnivall order.

contentParts.label: The Shipman's Tale
contentParts.summary: Fabliau of a merchant, his wife, and the monk Daun John.

contentParts.label: Shipman-Prioress Link
contentParts.summary: The Host's words after the Shipman's tale and summons to the Prioress.

contentParts.label: Prioress's Prologue
contentParts.summary: Marian invocation before the Prioress's miracle tale.

contentParts.label: The Prioress's Tale
contentParts.summary: Miracle of the Virgin centered on a murdered schoolboy; contains antisemitic narrative material.

contentParts.label: Host-Chaucer Link
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on Chaucer the pilgrim to tell.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
contentParts.summary: Interrupted parody of tail-rhyme romance told by Chaucer the pilgrim.

contentParts.label: Host's Interruption of Sir Thopas
contentParts.summary: The Host stops Sir Thopas and requests a different tale.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Melibee
contentParts.summary: Prose tale and debate on counsel, vengeance, and reconciliation.

contentParts.label: Monk's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host's words to the Monk and the Monk's definition of tragedy.

contentParts.label: The Monk's Tale
notes: The eighteen internal tragedy divisions are subdivisions of one conventional pilgrim tale, not eighteen counted tales.
contentParts.summary: Series of eighteen tragedies of falls from prosperity.

contentParts.label: Monk-Nun's Priest Link and Knight's Interruption
contentParts.summary: The Knight stops the Monk; the Host calls on the Nun's Priest.

contentParts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
contentParts.summary: Beast fable of Chauntecleer, Pertelote, and the fox.

contentParts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
contentParts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

contentParts.label: Second Nun's Prologue
contentParts.summary: Invocation and etymological praise of Saint Cecilia.

contentParts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
contentParts.summary: Saint's life of Cecilia.

contentParts.label: Canon's Yeoman Preamble
contentParts.summary: Arrival and departure of the Canon and the Yeoman's introductory exposure of alchemy.

contentParts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
contentParts.summary: Alchemy tale exposing a fraudulent canon.

contentParts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
contentParts.summary: Manciple head-link and tale.

contentParts.label: Manciple's Head-link
contentParts.summary: The Host's exchange with the Cook and call for the Manciple's tale.

contentParts.label: The Manciple's Tale
contentParts.summary: Moral tale of Phoebus and the crow.

contentParts.label: Group I, Fragment X
contentParts.summary: Parson head-link, Furnivall's analysis, prose tale, and Retraction.

contentParts.label: Parson's Head-link
contentParts.summary: The Host calls for the final tale and the Parson promises moral prose.

contentParts.label: Contents and Analysis of the Parson's Tale

locators.value: pp. 587, 587a, 587b, 588, and 588a; Parson text begins at p. 588b

contentParts.summary: Furnivall's omitted-from-running-text analytical contents preceding the tale.

contentParts.label: The Parson's Tale
contentParts.summary: Prose penitential treatise through the three parts of penitence.

contentParts.label: Retraction
notes: A distinct epilogue, not a third prose tale.
contentParts.summary: The closing prose retraction beginning with the maker taking leave.

contentParts.label: Furnivall Appendix Texts and Drawings
contentParts.summary: Separately sourced texts and drawings printed with the edition but not counted as Canterbury Tales core items.

contentParts.label: Spurious Gamelyn Link
notes: Not present in Ellesmere and not counted as a tale.
contentParts.summary: Two-line link transcribed from British Library Royal MS 18 C II.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Gamelyn
notes: Retained as appendix apparatus and excluded from the conventional count of twenty-four Chaucerian tales.
contentParts.summary: Nine-hundred-three-line non-Ellesmere tale transcribed from Royal MS 18 C II.

contentParts.label: Man of Law-Shipman Link
contentParts.summary: Twenty-eight-line link from Bodleian Library MS Arch. Selden B.14.

contentParts.label: Nun's Priest's End-link
contentParts.summary: Sixteen-line extract from Cambridge University Library MS Dd.4.24, fol. 195b.

contentParts.label: Cambridge Gg.4.27 Drawing Appendix
notes: Visual apparatus, not textual items.
contentParts.summary: Drawings of the Cook, Reeve, Wife, Pardoner, Monk, and Manciple, with six allegorical figures, reproduced from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27.

contentParts.label: Visual and Correction Apparatus
contentParts.summary: Edition-level teller drawings, manuscript miniature facsimiles, and the later correction leaf.

contentParts.label: Hooper Drawings of the 23 Tellers
contentParts.summary: Twenty-three drawings copied from the Ellesmere miniatures and cut on wood by W. H. Hooper.

contentParts.label: Facsimiles of the 23 Ellesmere Miniatures
contentParts.summary: Separate suite of twenty-three facsimiles of the manuscript's teller miniatures.

contentParts.label: Ewald Flügel's 1907 Corrections
contentParts.summary: Correction leaf for the Six-Text and separate Ellesmere print.

dateStatements.display: Begun probably in the late 1380s and worked on through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame; the date does not apply automatically to anonymous appendix texts.
This is a witness-production date, not a composition date.

scope.description: The ten-fragment Ellesmere-ordered Canterbury Tales core, not the non-Ellesmere appendix or modern edition apparatus and not necessarily the earliest attestation of every tale

dateStatements.display: Chaucer Society edition issued in eight parts, 1868-1884; final title page dated 1868-1879

scope.description: The exact edition-defined content and apparatus represented by agz8232

extent.description: Exactly twenty-four tales are counted: twenty-two verse tales and two prose tales. The seventy-five-part inventory also exposes all containers, links, prologues, interruptions, the prose Retraction, appendix texts, drawings, facsimiles, and correction apparatus. The XML contains 784 LG and 18,309 L elements in total. Excluding four appendix LG with 949 L and the rejected Clerk-link LG with seven L yields 779 verse groups and 17,353 verse-line elements in the Ellesmere-ordered core; these are encoding-derived metrics, not a new critical lineation.

formStatements.description: Predominantly verse, with the prose Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale; the Retraction is prose apparatus and is not counted as a third tale.

scope.description: The pilgrimage frame introduced in the General Prologue
Especially the General Prologue's social portraits
A constituent mode, serious and parodic
Constituent fabliaux and closely related tale modes

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin rubrics and quotations do not displace Middle English as the primary language; appendix spellings reflect their separate witnesses.
The twenty-four-tale count is semantic: prologues, links, interruptions, Retraction, apparatus, and the non-Ellesmere Tale of Gamelyn do not increment it.
Group letters and fragment numerals follow the Furnivall/Ellesmere presentation; their sequence is witness-specific and editorially mediated.
The Parson's Tale and Melibee are prose even where digital serialization uses line-like structures; visual apparatus uses textual_form other.
No reusable modeling problem beyond the project's current boundary and rights case lessons was discovered, so no shared skill file is changed on this research branch.

next_steps: Treat the Ellesmere/Furnivall sequence as a witness and editorial arrangement, not an authorial fact.
Preserve letter-group and fragment crosswalks in discovery displays.

subjects.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: A company of pilgrims traveling from Southwark toward Thomas Becket's shrine at Canterbury agree to tell stories. Chaucer's unfinished frame juxtaposes social portraits, competing voices, literary genres, moral argument, and performances ranging from romance, saint's life, and Marian miracle to fabliau, beast fable, prose instruction, and penitential sermon. This representation preserves the distinctive Ellesmere fragment order and Furnivall's edition-level apparatus.

The complete inventory has seventy-five parts: ten fragment/group containers; fifty-five core components containing exactly twenty-four counted tales; one appendix container with five non-counted appendix components; and one visual/correction container with three non-counted apparatus components. The appendix records Gamelyn and its link from Royal 18 C II, the Man of Law-Shipman link from Arch. Selden B.14, the Nun's Priest end-link from Dd.4.24, and drawings from Gg.4.27. The visual/correction layer records twenty-three Hooper teller cuts, twenty-three manuscript-miniature facsimiles, and Flügel's 1907 corrections.

This record catalogs Chaucer's composite work through one specific witness-and-edition representation. The medieval manuscript, Furnivall edition, U-M encoding and page images, repository XML, and third-party scans are separate lineage entities. Apparatus is retained for faithful discovery without silently treating Gamelyn, supplementary links, drawings, facsimile plates, or corrections as Chaucerian tales.

notes: The qualifying Ellesmere/Furnivall wording belongs to the cataloging-unit label, not to the conventional title of Chaucer's work.
Title of the immediate printed and electronic transmission layer.
